content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 8, 1994


Hadi Monsef
P.O. Box 1353
Alameda, CA  94501

                                        Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                             Our File No. I-94-278

Dear Ms. Monsef:

     You have requested informal assistance under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").

     You have asked if it is permissible for Mr. Richard
Armstrong, who is an employee of the Bay Area Rapid Transit
District ("BART"), to run for the BART board of directors.

     Unfortunately, we are unable to respond to your question.
The Fair Political Practices Commission can only give advice to a
public official whose duties are in question under the Act, or his
or her authorized representative.  (Section 83114(b).)  We cannot
give third party advice.  Since you are seeking advice on the
conduct of another individual and it does not appear that you have
been authorized to seek advice on his behalf, we are unable to
respond to your question.

     Enclosed, however, is a copy of an advice letter rendered to
a public official who posed a similar question.  Perhaps this can
provide you with some guidance.

     If you have any questions, please feel free to contact the
Commission's Legal Division at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Jeanette E. Turvill
                                            Political Reform Consultant
                                            Legal Division

SGC/JET/jt
Enclosure


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

sections.question: Is it permissible for Mr. Richard Armstrong, who is an employee of the Bay Area Rapid Transit District ("BART"), to run for the BART board of directors?
sections.conclusion: The Fair Political Practices Commission can only give advice to a public official whose duties are in question under the Act, or his or her authorized representative. We cannot give third party advice. Since you are seeking advice on the conduct of another individual and it does not appear that you have been authorized to seek advice on his behalf, we are unable to respond to your question.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether an employee of BART may run for a position on the BART board of directors under California's conflict-of-interest laws.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined to provide advice because the requestor is seeking guidance on another individual's conduct and is not an authorized representative of that person. The FPPC can only provide advice to public officials or their authorized representatives, not third parties.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Declination letter with clear question and conclusion sections. FPPC explains jurisdictional limitation on providing advice only to public officials or authorized representatives, not third parties. An enclosure with a similar prior advice letter is referenced.

QUESTION: Is it permissible for Mr. Richard Armstrong, who is an employee of the Bay Area Rapid Transit District ("BART"), to run for the BART board of directors?

CONCLUSION: The Fair Political Practices Commission can only give advice to a public official whose duties are in question under the Act, or his or her authorized representative. We cannot give third party advice. Since you are seeking advice on the conduct of another individual and it does not appear that you have been authorized to seek advice on his behalf, we are unable to respond to your question.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declined to provide informal advice on whether a BART employee may run for the BART board, citing lack of authority to give third-party advice under Section 83114(b) of the Political Reform Act.
